variables.content: Because direct thermodynamic-limit extrapolation of the discrete-angle DMC-determined Fermi liquid parameters proved unreliable, a set of “best available” numerical recommendations for the Fermi liquid parameters was assembled by combining independently published QMC results for related quantities: quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ obtained from DMC energy-band calculations and smooth QMC-based parameterizations of the ground-state correlation energy.  Using the literature effective masses together with compressibility and spin-susceptibility values derived from the total-energy parameterizations and the Landau relations yields recommended values such as, for the paramagnetic 2D HEG: at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}^{s}=-0.495(2)$, $F_{0}^{a}=-0.346(2)$, $F_{1}^{s}=-0.053(3)$; at $r_{s}=5$ and $10$ analogous recommended values are provided; and for the ferromagnetic 2D HEG recommended values include at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}=-0.428(2)$ and $\bar{F}_1=-0.159(3)$.  These assembled values combine ground-state energy parameterizations and published effective masses to provide pragmatic numerical recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable.

Let $E_c^{\mathrm{QMC}}(r_s)$ denote the correlation energy per particle for the homogeneous electron gas at Wigner–Seitz radius $r_s$ as given by widely used quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) parametrizations for three-dimensional and two-dimensional electron gases (specifically, the parametrizations of Ortiz, Harris, and Ballone for 3D and of Attaccalite et al. for 2D); these QMC parametrizations are used as accurate reference values for the "exact" correlation energy $E_c(r_s)$ of the HEG so that numerical reproduction of $E_c^{\mathrm{QMC}}(r_s)$ by an approximate functional is treated as reproduction of the exact correlation energy for the purpose of assessing whether $E_c$, $t_c$, and $\tilde{E}_c$ are numerically matched by the power functional.
      Ortiz, Harris, and Ballone, Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 5317 (1999); Attaccalite et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 256601 (2002)

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

Let the correlation energy per electron $E_{c}(r_{s})$ be modeled by the analytic functional form
$E_{c}=A_{0}+(B_{0}r_{s}+C_{0}r_{s}^{2}+D_{0}r_{s}^{3})\log\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{E_{0}r_{s}+F_{0}r_{s}^{3/2}+G_{0}r_{s}^{2}+H_{0}r_{s}^{3}}\right)$.
The proposition is that this parametrization is flexible and physically motivated enough to represent the DMC-computed correlation energy of the paramagnetic 2D HEG with acceptable fidelity over the density range to which it is fitted, and that extrapolations or interpolations made using the fitted parameters are reasonably reliable within the fitted domain.
      C. Attaccalite et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 256601 (2002).

Let g_{Att}(r) denote the Attaccalite et al. parametrized QMC PDFs used as primary targets and let g_{Drum}(r) denote the numerical tabulations provided by Drummond et al.; the proposition is that differences between these available QMC datasets (for example, discrepancies between g_{Att} and g_{Drum} at large r_{s}) can lead to quantitatively different extracted bridge functions B(r) and hence to differences in subsequent CHNC predictions (including possible implications for marginal phase-transition behavior), so conclusions drawn from a single QMC input set inherit uncertainty from inter-dataset discrepancies.
      N. D. Drummond and R. J. Needs, Phys. Rev. B 79, 085414 (2009)

The exchange-correlation energy functional E_{\mathrm{xc}}[n_{\uparrow},n_{\downarrow}] entering the LSDA linear-response kernel for the quantum-well electron system with areal density n_{2D}=4.4×10^{11} cm^{-2} and spin polarization ζ≤0.35 was taken from the parameterization fitted to Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) results for the strictly two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas reported by Attaccalite et al. (2002), and this strictly two-dimensional QMC parameterization was assumed to represent exchange-correlation effects for the experimental quasi-two-dimensional quantum well of finite thickness w=150 Å despite the presence of subband-confinement form factors and possible non-uniform transverse density profiles.
      C. Attaccalite, S. Moroni, P. Gori-Giorgi, and G. B. Bachelet, Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 256601 (2002).

Let D_{QMC} denote the finite set of numerical values for γ(r_s) produced by the QMC calculations of Drummond et al.; then performing a least-squares fit (or a visually comparable fit) of the polynomial model γ(r_s)=1+1.23 r_s + p r_s^2 + r_s^3/3 to D_{QMC} yields a best-fit quadratic coefficient p = −0.22 as reported for the Drummond QMC data, and the graphical agreement between the fitted polynomial and the QMC points (as presented in the QMC plot) supports using p = −0.22 as a reliable summary parametrization of those QMC results over the r_s values included in D_{QMC}.

When the HEG limit ($\nabla\rho=0$) of the model's coupling-constant potential expressions $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\sigma\sigma',\lambda=1}$ is fitted by least-squares to reference Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) values of $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\sigma\sigma',\lambda=1}$ across metallic densities $r_{\mathrm{s}}=1,2,\dots,10$, the best-fit constants are $F_{\alpha\beta}=2.1070$ and $F_{\alpha\alpha}=2.6422$, and the mean absolute percentage errors (MAPE) of the fit relative to the QMC reference are 5.0% for the opposite-spin component and 12.0% for the parallel-spin component, yielding a total $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\lambda=1}$ MAPE of 4.6%; these numerical fitting outcomes were obtained by fitting the model HEG expressions to QMC-derived $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\sigma\sigma',\lambda=1}$ values.
      P. Gori-Giorgi, F. Sacchetti, and G. Bachelet, Phys. Rev. B 61, 7353 (2000) [QMC HEG reference data]

Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) calculations of the homogeneous one-dimensional electron gas interacting via the soft-Coulomb potential were performed at a finite but sufficiently dense set of density-parameter values $r_s$ that cover the interval of electron densities occurring in the finite atomic and molecular systems studied (the one-dimensional atoms H, He, Li, Be and the H$_2$ molecule); fitting the seven independent parameters per polarization in the analytic functional form above to these QMC data determines the parameters $A,B,C,D,E,\alpha,\beta,m$ so that the resulting parametrized correlation energy $\epsilon_c(r_s,\zeta)$ reproduces the QMC energies within the reported average errors across that density range. [27][28]

The BBC1 correction to Müller-like functionals, defined by the occupation-number dependent kernel that distinguishes strongly and weakly occupied orbitals, reproduces the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) correlation energy in good agreement with quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) across a wide range of Wigner–Seitz radii $r_s$ and restores a discontinuity in the momentum distribution $n(k)$ at the Fermi momentum $k_F$; nevertheless, EKT quasiparticle dispersions computed from BBC1 one- and two-body reduced density matrix inputs produce valence-band dispersions that closely resemble Hartree–Fock dispersions rather than the QMC/EKT@QMC reference and only partially remove the artificial EKT band gap in the HEG.
